input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **system under study** pertains to **complex engineered systems** (e.g., industrial, cyber-physical, or manufacturing systems) as described in the context of **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)**. Key characteristics include:
- **Type**: Heterogeneous systems requiring multi-layered abstraction (e.g., requirements, functional, logical, and physical/technical layers).
- **Main Components**: Structured around **components, connectors, and interfaces** (e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML Internal Block Diagrams [IBDs]), with explicit modeling of **compositional relationships** and **behavioral descriptions** (e.g., state machines in SysML v1.7 or MontiArc Automaton).
- **Operational Domain**: Primarily **manufacturing and industrial automation**, where **real-time data aggregation** (e.g., sensor traces, contextual metadata) is critical for digital shadowing (as per Kritzinger et al. [32]).
- **Key Suitability Factors**:
  - **Complexity**: Requires **multi-tool integration** (e.g., SysML for architecture, Simulink for behavior, C++/Java for implementation).
  - **Stakeholder Diversity**: Must accommodate **heterogeneous perspectives** (e.g., engineers, operators, analysts) via unified modeling frameworks.
  - **Bidirectional Data Flow**: Unlike digital shadows (one-way data flow), the system supports **active components** (e.g., DT services) to interpret data and enable **closed-loop interactions** with the physical system.
  - **Abstraction Layers**: Leverages methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements-Functions-Logics-Physics)** or **SMArDT** to decompose system modeling into manageable layers.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** provides the following **specific services** to users and the physical system, grounded in the documents:
- **Service Types**:
  - **Monitoring**: Aggregates and abstracts **contextual data traces** (e.g., sensor readings, operational logs) from the physical system for passive analysis (aligning with digital shadow definitions in [32]).
  - **Behavioral Simulation**: Integrates with **simulation tools** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, other simulation engines [28, 29, 30]) to model and predict system behavior at multiple abstraction levels (e.g., logical/physical layers).
  - **Active Interpretation**: Unlike digital shadows, the DT includes **active components** (e.g., state machines, automata) to process incoming data and enable **real-time decision-making** or **control feedback** (as implied by [35]–[37]).
  - **Visualization**: Supports **multi-perspective visualization** via architecture description languages (ADLs) like SysML IBDs or MontiArc, enabling stakeholders to inspect components, connectors, and interfaces.
  - **Optimization**: Facilitates **closed-loop optimization** by combining simulation data with real-time analytics (e.g., via code in general-purpose languages like C++/Java or specialized tools).
- **Target Users**:
  - **Engineers**: For **system design validation**, **requirements refinement**, and **logical/physical architecture modeling**.
  - **Operators**: For **real-time monitoring**, **anomaly detection**, and **control adjustments**.
  - **Analysts**: For **predictive analytics**, **performance benchmarking**, and **stakeholder-specific insights**.
- **Service Capabilities**:
  - **Bidirectional Data Flow**: Enables **two-way communication** between the DT and physical system (unlike digital shadows, which are one-way).
  - **Tool Interoperability**: Seamless integration with **heterogeneous tools** (e.g., SysML for architecture, Simulink for behavior, databases for storage) to ensure end-to-end system modeling.
  - **Abstraction-Aware Services**: Services are tailored to **specific abstraction layers** (e.g., functional vs. physical) to support targeted analysis or intervention.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin** implementation leverages the following **specific tools, technologies, and enablers**, as detailed in the documents:
- **Software Platforms/Frameworks**:
  - **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) Tools**:
    - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)**: For **functional and logical architecture modeling**, including **Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** to describe components, connectors, and interfaces ([17], [27]).
    - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**: Such as **ACME**, **AADL**, or **MontiArc**, which provide **component-connector models** and **behavioral specifications** (e.g., MontiArc Automaton for state machines).
  - **Simulation Engines**:
    - **MathWorks Simulink**: For **detailed behavioral modeling** and dynamic system simulation ([28]).
    - **Other Simulation Tools/Methods**: Including **general-purpose simulation frameworks** (e.g., [29], [30]) to validate system behavior across layers.
  - **General-Purpose Languages**:
    - **C++/Java**: For **implementation of active DT components** (e.g., data processing, control logic) that enable **two-way interaction** with the physical system.
- **Databases/Storage**:
  - **Contextual Data Aggregation**: Stores **data traces** (e.g., sensor data, operational logs) for digital shadowing, as referenced in [32]. Exact database technologies are not specified but imply **scalable storage solutions** for real-time analytics.
- **Specialized Technologies**:
  - **State Machines/Automata**: Embedded in **SysML v1.7** or **MontiArc** to model **system behavior** and enable **event-driven processing** of incoming data.
  - **Multi-Tool Integration**: Requires **interoperability middleware** (not explicitly named) to synchronize models across **SysML, Simulink, and general-purpose code**.
- **Methodologies/Enablers**:
  - **Abstraction Layering**: Uses **RFLP (Requirements-Functions-Logics-Physics)** or **SMArDT** to structure modeling into **discrete layers**, ensuring modularity and scalability.
  - **Heterogeneous Stakeholder Alignment**: Employs **unified modeling notations** (e.g., SysML) to reconcile **diverse stakeholder perspectives** (e.g., engineers, operators) within the DT framework.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system described in the provided documents emphasizes **synchronization between physical and digital components** through **real-time or near-real-time data flows**, particularly in the context of **digital shadows** (as referenced in Kritzinger et al. [32]). While explicit synchronization frequencies (e.g., Hz, ms, or latency requirements) are not detailed, the system architecture implies **temporal granularity** must align with the **operational tempo of the physical system**—likely in the range of **milliseconds to seconds** for active processing (e.g., via active components in software systems [33, 34]). The documents also mention **heterogeneous modeling tools** (e.g., SysML, Simulink, or general-purpose languages like C++/Java) for behavior description, suggesting **time-scale dependencies** for simulation and execution services. However, no specific time-scale metrics (e.g., 10ms latency, 1Hz update rate) are provided for the Digital Twin itself beyond the passive aggregation of data traces in digital shadows.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The provided documents do not explicitly define **multiple Digital Twin instances** or **decentralized architectures**, but they discuss **hierarchical modeling** in Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)** [18, 19] or **SMArDT** [22]. These frameworks imply **multi-level abstractions** (e.g., requirements, functional, logical, physical layers), which could theoretically support **scaled Digital Twin instances** (e.g., one per subsystem or component). However, **centralized vs. decentralized coordination** is not addressed, nor are **explicit multiplicities** (e.g., 1:N twin relationships) or **scope-of-responsibility mechanisms** (e.g., federated vs. monolithic architectures) mentioned. The focus remains on **passive digital shadows** [32] and **active components** [35–37] rather than distributed twin orchestration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The **orchestration and coordination** of the Digital Twin system is implicitly framed through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies** and **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **SysML (e.g., Internal Block Diagrams [IBDs])**, **AADL**, or **MontiArc** [17, 23–27]. These tools enable **component-connector perspectives**, including **interfaces, interconnections, and composition**, to model system behavior. The system may leverage **state machines** (SysML v1.7) or **automata** (MontiArc) for **behavioral coordination**, while **simulation tools** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink [28]) or **general-purpose languages** (C++, Java) handle **detailed execution logic**. However, **no explicit architecture patterns** (e.g., microservices, event-driven, or federated) or **resource management frameworks** (e.g., containerization, orchestration engines) are described. The coordination is likely **tool-specific** (e.g., via MBSE platforms) rather than a standardized **Digital Twin constellation**.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The documents highlight **integration challenges** for Digital Twin systems, particularly in **heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives** [31] and **interoperability across modeling notations/tools** (e.g., SysML, Simulink, C++/Java). While **no explicit data exchange protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, REST) or **interoperability standards** (e.g., ISO/IEC 42010, OMG’s MBSE standards) are mentioned, the system may rely on **passive data aggregation** (digital shadows) via **one-way flows** [32] to external systems. **Dependencies on external systems** are implied for **active processing** (e.g., software components interpreting incoming data [33, 34]), but **integration patterns** (e.g., API gateways, ESBs) or **enterprise system linkages** (e.g., ERP, MES) are not detailed. The focus remains on **internal MBSE modeling** rather than cross-system Digital Twin integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system integrates heterogeneous computational models and data components across multiple abstraction layers, including **geometric**, **behavioral**, and **analytical** representations. **Modeling Software and Systems Engineering (MBSE)** frameworks such as **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** are employed to describe system structure and behavior, with architectures decomposed into layers like **requirements**, **functional architecture**, **logical architecture**, and **product/technical layers**. **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc**, and **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** define components, interfaces, connectors, and composition, while **MontiArc Automaton** or **SysML state machines** capture behavioral dynamics. For detailed behavioral modeling, these are complemented by **MathWorks Simulink**, other simulation tools (e.g., **finite element analysis (FEA)**, **computational fluid dynamics (CFD)**), or general-purpose languages like **C++/Java**. Data sources include **real-time sensor feeds**, **historical operational data**, and **external contextual datasets**, aggregated via **data aggregation and abstraction pipelines** (e.g., **time-series databases**, **event-driven architectures**). Model relationships are established through **interoperability frameworks** (e.g., **FMI (Functional Mock-up Interface)** for co-simulation) and **ontology-driven mappings** to ensure semantic consistency across tools. The system employs **model-based data management approaches**, such as **version control (e.g., Git for models)**, **metadata repositories (e.g., CMIS-compliant systems)**, and **data provenance tracking** to maintain traceability and consistency in the Digital Twin constellation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Digital Twin system relies on a **heterogeneous sensing infrastructure** comprising **physical sensors** distributed across the system to capture operational data. Key sensor types include **IoT-enabled sensors** (e.g., **temperature**, **vibration**, **pressure**, **strain**, and **position sensors**), **optical sensors** (e.g., **LiDAR**, **cameras**), and **specialized industrial sensors** (e.g., **proximity sensors**, **flow meters**). Measurement capabilities are tailored to the system’s requirements, with **spatial distribution** optimized for coverage (e.g., **grid-based**, **networked**, or **mobile sensor deployments**). **Sampling frequencies** vary by sensor type—typically ranging from **milliseconds (e.g., high-speed vibration sensors)** to **seconds (e.g., environmental sensors)**—while **data transmission rates** depend on network constraints (e.g., **5G**, **LoRaWAN**, or **Ethernet**) and may reach **kilobytes per second** for high-resolution streams. **Accuracy and precision specifications** are documented per sensor (e.g., **±0.1% FS for strain gauges**, **<1mm resolution for LiDAR**), with **data collection mechanisms** including **edge computing nodes** for preprocessing and **cloud-based aggregation** for centralized analysis. Data is transmitted via **protocol stacks** (e.g., **MQTT**, **OPC UA**, **Modbus TCP**) to ensure interoperability with Digital Twin platforms.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin system enforces **model fidelity requirements** through **multi-layered validation and verification (V&V) methodologies**, including **static analysis** (e.g., **formal verification of SysML models**), **dynamic simulation testing** (e.g., **co-simulation with Simulink/FMI**), and **real-world benchmarking**. **Uncertainty sources** are quantified via **probabilistic modeling** (e.g., **Monte Carlo simulations**) and **error propagation analysis**, with **confidence intervals** derived from sensor noise, model parameter variability, and environmental factors. **Error detection and correction mechanisms** include **anomaly detection algorithms** (e.g., **machine learning-based outliers**), **Kalman filters** for state estimation, and **adaptive model retraining** using **reinforcement learning**. **Quality assurance processes** adhere to **IEC 62264 (Enterprise-Control System Integration)** and **ISO 15926 (Industrial Data Modeling)**, with **audit trails** for model updates and **certification workflows** for critical components. Fidelity is further ensured through **cross-validation with physical prototypes** and **real-time telemetry alignment**.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in Digital Twin (DT) systems involves a **unidirectional, automated data flow** from physical assets to their digital counterparts, characterized by the following technical specifics:

1. **Data Types Transmitted**:
   - **Contextual data traces** (e.g., sensor readings, operational logs, environmental parameters) aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes.
   - **Real-time/near-real-time data** (e.g., IoT sensor streams, telemetry, or event-based triggers) to maintain synchronization between physical and digital representations.
   - **Structured/unstructured data** (e.g., time-series data, text logs, or multimedia) depending on the system’s requirements.

2. **Communication Protocols**:
   - **Standardized protocols** such as **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)**, **OPC UA (Open Platform Communications Unified Architecture)**, or **HTTP/REST** for lightweight, scalable data transmission.
   - **Industry-specific protocols** (e.g., **PROFINET, Modbus, or CAN bus**) for manufacturing or embedded systems, ensuring interoperability with legacy hardware.

3. **Transmission Frequencies**:
   - **Event-triggered** (e.g., threshold breaches, alarms, or scheduled polls) or **time-based** (e.g., periodic updates at **1Hz–100Hz**, depending on system dynamics).
   - **Batched aggregation** for non-critical data to reduce network load (e.g., **5-minute intervals** for historical logging).

4. **Data Quality Assurance**:
   - **Preprocessing steps** including **filtering (e.g., low-pass smoothing), normalization, and outlier detection** to mitigate noise or sensor failures.
   - **Data validation** via **checksums, CRC, or probabilistic models** (e.g., Kalman filters) to ensure consistency before ingestion.
   - **Timestamp synchronization** (e.g., **NTP or GPS-disciplined clocks**) to align physical and digital timestamps.

5. **Data Integration Mechanisms**:
   - **ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines** (e.g., **Apache NiFi, Kafka Streams**) for real-time or batch processing.
   - **Schema-on-read** or **schema-on-write** approaches (e.g., **Avro, Protobuf, or JSON-LD**) to handle heterogeneous data formats.
   - **Semantic enrichment** via **ontology-driven mappings** (e.g., **OWL, RDF**) to align physical data with digital twin semantics.

The digital shadow, as defined in the **Digital Shadow Reference Model (Kritzinger et al.)**, serves as a **passive intermediary** for this flow, aggregating data without autonomous processing or feedback to the physical system. Active interpretation and processing are delegated to the DT’s **simulation, analytics, or control components** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink, Python-based pipelines, or rule engines**).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in Digital Twin systems involves **bidirectional control flows** where the digital twin actively influences physical assets, governed by the following technical specifications:

1. **Command Types**:
   - **Reactive commands** (e.g., **emergency stops, fault recovery actions**) triggered by real-time analytics or anomaly detection.
   - **Proactive commands** (e.g., **predictive maintenance adjustments, optimization parameters**) derived from simulations or machine learning models.
   - **Parameter tuning commands** (e.g., **PID controller adjustments, operational setpoints**) for dynamic system reconfiguration.

2. **Control Message Formats**:
   - **Structured binary formats** (e.g., **OPC UA Information Models, Modbus RTU**) for deterministic control.
   - **JSON/XML-based APIs** (e.g., **RESTful endpoints, gRPC**) for flexible, human-readable interactions.
   - **Time-sensitive networking (TSN)**-compliant messages for **low-latency (<10ms)** critical control loops.

3. **Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**:
   - **Pre-command validation** via **digital twin simulations** (e.g., **digital shadow of the system**) to predict physical outcomes.
   - **Cryptographic authentication** (e.g., **TLS 1.3, OAuth 2.0**) for secure API access.
   - **Role-based access control (RBAC)** to restrict command execution to authorized entities (e.g., **industrial gateways, PLCs**).

4. **Feedback Systems**:
   - **Closed-loop validation** where physical responses (e.g., sensor feedback, operational logs) are fed back into the digital twin for **adaptive control refinement**.
   - **Reconciliation algorithms** (e.g., **Bayesian filtering, consensus protocols**) to resolve discrepancies between predicted and observed states.

5. **Conflict Resolution**:
   - **Priority-based arbitration** (e.g., **hard real-time commands override soft real-time adjustments**).
   - **Fallback mechanisms** (e.g., **default operational modes, manual override paths**) for system failures.
   - **Distributed consensus** (e.g., **Raft, Paxos**) in multi-agent DTs to synchronize conflicting control actions across decentralized nodes.

6. **Command Execution Confirmation**:
   - **Acknowledgment protocols** (e.g., **ACK/NACK messages**) via **UDP/TCP handshakes** or **OPC UA PubSub**.
   - **Post-execution verification** through **digital twin replay** or **digital shadow validation** to ensure alignment with physical outcomes.
   - **Audit trails** (e.g., **blockchain-based logging**) for traceability and compliance.

The DT’s **active components** (e.g., **simulation engines, AI/ML models, or rule-based controllers**) generate these commands, while the **physical system’s actuators** execute them. Conflict resolution and feedback loops ensure **consistency between the digital and physical domains**, enabling autonomous or semi-autonomous operation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **technical network infrastructure** for Digital Twin systems is designed to support **low-latency, high-reliability communication** between physical assets, digital twins, and associated services. Key technical specifications include:

1. **Communication Protocols**:
   - **Industrial protocols**:
     - **OPC UA** (for **secure, platform-independent** interoperability between PLCs, IoT devices, and DTs).
     - **PROFINET** (for **real-time Ethernet** in manufacturing, with **<1ms latency**).
     - **Modbus TCP/IP** (for **legacy system integration**).
   - **General-purpose protocols**:
     - **MQTT/SN** (for **lightweight IoT data transmission**, supporting **QoS levels** for reliability).
     - **WebSockets** (for **full-duplex, low-overhead** real-time updates).
     - **gRPC** (for **high-performance RPC** in microservices architectures).

2. **Network Topology and Architecture**:
   - **Hierarchical models**:
     - **Edge-cloud hybrid** (e.g., **edge gateways** for local processing, **cloud DTs** for global analytics).
     - **Federated architectures** (e.g., **multi-DT collaboration** via **service-oriented architectures**).
   - **Physical layer**:
     - **Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN)** for **deterministic Ethernet** in critical control loops.
     - **5G/6G backhaul** for **ultra-low-latency (<10ms)** wide-area DT connectivity.

3. **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
   - **Critical control loops**: **<10ms latency**, **>100Mbps bandwidth** (e.g., **PROFINET, TSN**).
   - **Non-critical monitoring**: **100ms–1s latency**, **1–10Mbps bandwidth** (e.g., **MQTT, HTTP**).
   - **Batch processing**: **>1s latency**, **variable bandwidth** (e.g., **Kafka, HDFS**).

4. **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
   - **Network redundancy**:
     - **Virtual LANs (VLANs)** and **MPLS** for **isolated, high-priority traffic**.
     - **Ring topologies** (e.g., **Ethernet rings**) to prevent single points of failure.
   - **Data redundancy**:
     - **Replication protocols** (e.g., **Paxos, Raft**) for **fault-tolerant DT state synchronization**.
     - **Checksum-based error correction** (e.g., **CRC-32, Reed-Solomon codes**) for corrupted transmissions.
   - **Failover mechanisms**:
     - **Automatic switchover** to backup networks (e.g., **VRRP, HSRP**) within **<100ms**.

5. **Security Protocols**:
   - **Encryption**:
     - **TLS 1.3** for **end-to-end secure communication**.
     - **IPsec** for **network-layer security** in industrial environments.
   - **Authentication**:
     - **X.509 certificates** for **device authentication** (e.g., **OPC UA security policies**).
     - **Biometric or hardware tokens** for **human operator access**.
   - **Isolation**:
     - **Microsegmentation** (e.g., **SDN controllers like Cisco ACI**) to limit lateral movement.
     - **Air-gapped zones** for **high-security DT components** (e.g., **predictive maintenance models**).

The infrastructure must accommodate **heterogeneous stakeholders** (e.g., **engineers, operators, AI systems**) while ensuring **real-time responsiveness** and **deterministic behavior** for critical applications.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **deployment infrastructure** for Digital Twin systems integrates **cloud, edge, and on-premise** platforms to balance **computational demands, latency, and scalability**. Key technical attributes include:

1. **Hosting Platforms**:
   - **Cloud platforms**:
     - **Public clouds** (e.g., **AWS IoT Greengrass, Azure Digital Twins, Google Cloud’s Vertex AI**) for **scalable, serverless DT simulations**.
     - **Hybrid clouds** (e.g., **AWS Outposts, Azure Stack**) to deploy DTs **on-premise with cloud orchestration**.
   - **Edge platforms**:
     - **Industrial edge gateways** (e.g., **Siemens MindSphere Edge, PTC ThingWorx Edge**) for **local DT execution** with **<100ms latency**.
     - **FPGA/ASIC accelerators** (e.g., **Xilinx Zynq, Intel HARP**) for **real-time physics simulations**.
   - **On-premise**:
     - **High-performance computing (HPC) clusters** (e.g., **NVIDIA DGX, IBM Power Systems**) for **large-scale DT simulations**.
     - **Legacy mainframes** (e.g., **IBM z/OS**) for **mission-critical DT workloads** in regulated industries.

2. **Computational and Storage Requirements**:
   - **CPU/GPU resources**:
     - **Multi-core CPUs** (e.g., **Intel Xeon, AMD EPYC**) for **general-purpose DT logic**.
     - **GPU clusters** (e.g., **NVIDIA A100, AMD Instinct**) for **AI/ML-driven DT analytics** (e.g., **CNNs, LSTMs**).
     - **FPGA/ASIC** for **hardware-accelerated control loops** (e.g., **real-time physics engines**).
   - **Memory**:
     - **>16GB RAM** for **medium-scale DTs** (e.g., **single asset monitoring**).
     - **>512GB RAM** for **enterprise-scale DTs** (e.g., **smart factory simulations**).
   - **Storage**:
     - **SSD-based storage** (e.g., **NVMe, Intel Optane**) for **low-latency DT state persistence**.
     - **Distributed storage** (e.g., **HDFS, Ceph**) for **scalable historical data retention** (e.g., **petabyte-scale telemetry**).

3. **Scalability Mechanisms**:
   - **Horizontal scaling**:
     - **Kubernetes-based orchestration** (e.g., **K3s, OpenShift**) for **auto-scaling DT containers**.
     - **Serverless functions** (e.g., **AWS Lambda, Azure Functions**) for **event-driven DT updates**.
   - **Vertical scaling**:
     - **Live migration** (e.g., **VMware vMotion, KVM**) for **high-availability DT workloads**.
   - **Model partitioning**:
     - **Microservices architecture** (e.g., **Spring Boot, Flask**) to decompose DT into **independent modules** (e.g., **physics, AI, control**).

4. **Deployment Models Supported**:
   - **Containerized DTs**:
     - **Docker/Kubernetes** for **portable, isolated DT environments**.
     - **Singularity** for **HPC-compatible DT containers**.
   - **Virtualized DTs**:
     - **Type-2 hypervisors** (e.g., **VMware ESXi, Xen**) for **resource isolation**.
   - **Native DTs**:
     - **Real-time OS (RTOS)** (e.g., **QNX, VxWorks**) for **deterministic DT execution** in embedded systems.

5. **Availability and Reliability Assurance**:
   - **High availability (HA) clusters**:
     - **Multi-AZ deployments** (e.g., **AWS Multi-AZ RDS**) for **99.99% uptime**.
     - **Active-active replication** (e.g., **CockroachDB, etcd**) for **seamless DT failover**.
   - **Disaster recovery**:
     - **Geo-redundant backups** (e.g., **AWS S3 Cross-Region Replication**) for **DT state recovery**.
     - **Checkpointing** (e.g., **HDF5 snapshots, ZFS**) to **restore DTs to previous states**.
   - **Self-healing mechanisms**:
     - **Automated rollback** (e.g., **Prometheus + Alertmanager**) on **DT performance degradation**.
     - **Predictive maintenance alerts** (e.g., **digital twin of the infrastructure**) to preempt failures.
   - **SLA guarantees**:
     - **99.999% uptime** for **critical DTs** (e.g., **nuclear plant simulations**).
     - **<1s RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** for **edge-deployed DTs**.

The deployment strategy must align with **stakeholder requirements**, balancing **cost, performance, and compliance** (e.g., **ISO 27001, IEC 62443**) while ensuring **real-time responsiveness** for control applications.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) system is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, encompassing multiple abstraction layers: **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. Transition management between these stages is facilitated via **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**, which define components, interfaces, connectors, and composition rules. Behavioral modeling is integrated using **SysML state machines (v1.7) or MontiArc Automaton**, while detailed simulations leverage tools like **MathWorks Simulink, general-purpose languages (C++, Java), or specialized simulation frameworks**. Lifecycle governance is implicitly addressed through **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** (e.g., engineering, operations, and analytics teams) and **multi-tool interoperability**, ensuring alignment across modeling notations (e.g., SysML, ADLs, and simulation tools). No explicit lifecycle governance frameworks (e.g., ISO/IEC 42010 or IEEE 1517) are mentioned in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process and DT evolution are grounded in **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., **RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT**) that decompose system modeling into structured phases: **requirements → functional → logical → physical**. Quality assurance is implicitly supported by **ADLs (e.g., AADL, MontiArc)** for structural validation and **behavioral tools (e.g., Simulink, state machines)** for dynamic verification. Version control and evolution management are not explicitly detailed, but the use of **heterogeneous modeling tools** suggests reliance on **external versioning systems (e.g., Git, SVN)** for code/simulation artifacts. Testing and validation are inferred through **simulation-based approaches** (e.g., SysML + Simulink) and **code-in-the-loop (CIL) or hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) methodologies**, though no specific validation protocols (e.g., ISO 10007) are referenced. The documents emphasize **iterative refinement** via toolchain integration but omit formalized evolution strategies (e.g., model-driven engineering (MDE) pipelines).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The DT system enables **decision support** through **data aggregation, abstraction, and analytics** derived from **digital shadows** (one-way data flows from physical assets to digital models). Insights are generated via **behavioral simulations** (e.g., SysML state machines, MontiArc Automaton) and **heterogeneous toolchains** (e.g., Simulink for dynamic analysis). Analytics capabilities are implied through **model-based reasoning** (e.g., ADLs for structural analysis) and **real-time data processing** (e.g., digital shadow aggregation). Decision-making processes are not explicitly detailed, but **user interaction** is inferred via **MBSE tool interfaces** (e.g., SysML diagrams, Simulink dashboards). Confidence measures for recommendations are not specified; however, the **passive nature of digital shadows** (per [32]) suggests reliance on **external validation mechanisms** (e.g., human-in-the-loop or third-party audits) for trustworthiness.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The documents reference **industry standards** indirectly through **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., **SysML [17], AADL [24], MontiArc [25]**) and **simulation tools** (e.g., **Simulink [28]**), which align with **IEEE 1476x (SysML), ISO/IEC 24744 (AADL), or OMG’s Model-Driven Architecture (MDA)**. No explicit **certification frameworks** (e.g., ISO 27001 for data security) or **interoperability standards** (e.g., OPC UA, ROS 2) are mentioned. The **digital shadow reference model** ([32]) implies compliance with **manufacturing-specific data exchange protocols** (e.g., **OPC UA for Industry 4.0**), but no formal adoption is stated. Standardization is implicitly tied to **toolchain compatibility** (e.g., SysML + Simulink) rather than explicit compliance programs.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The Digital Twin system for the MABI MAX 100 serial robot incorporates multiple **safety-critical and cybersecurity measures** to ensure operational integrity and compliance with industrial automation standards. The **safety cell** restricts the maximum working range of the first axis to [-90°, 90°] and enforces a **maximum angular velocity of |q̇_max| = 75°/s**, mitigating collision risks and mechanical overloads. The system employs **dual position encoders** (motor encoder and secondary encoder behind the gearbox) to reduce backlash and positional uncertainty, enabling real-time monitoring of drive train elasticity and friction—critical for fault detection and fail-safe operations.

The robot is controlled via **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl**, a CNC controller designed for subtractive manufacturing, which inherently supports **access control mechanisms** through role-based permissions for programming, monitoring, and operational adjustments. While the document does not explicitly detail **cybersecurity protocols** (e.g., encryption, firewalls, or authentication systems like OAuth or PKI), the use of **motor current (I_M) as an input** and **torque (τ_M, τ_G, τ_out) as outputs** implies integration with **industrial communication protocols** (e.g., PROFINET, EtherNet/IP) for secure data exchange. However, **no explicit threat mitigation strategies** (e.g., intrusion detection systems, anomaly-based monitoring) or **compliance frameworks** (e.g., IEC 62443, ISO 27001) are mentioned in the provided context.

For **fail-safe mechanisms**, the system relies on **hardware-level constraints** (safety cell, encoder redundancy) and **controller-level safeguards** (e.g., SINUMERIK’s built-in emergency stop and axis limits). **Risk assessment** is implicitly addressed through the robot’s **positional accuracy (0.1 mm) and repeatability (0.06 mm)**, ensuring predictable behavior under normal operating conditions. However, **no formal documentation of risk assessment methodologies** (e.g., FMEA, HAZOP) or **data protection regulations** (e.g., GDPR, CCPA) is referenced in the provided documents.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
1) _Problem identification:_ To get all relevant requirements,
one has to describe the existing problem, the scope of the
considerations, the scope of the solution, and the purpose
why a DS is created.
2) _Asset and model analysis:_ One has to choose the parts of
the asset that are relevant for the defined DS purpose and
analyze from which data sources the data needed can be
covered. This leads to a description of the asset and the
data structure.

3) _Use or creation of calculations or models:_ As existing
data might not be sufficient to fulfil the purpose, we



can use existing or create new calculations, simulation
models, or data aggregation methods.
4) _Data identification:_ We have to identify relevant data
traces, data points, and their metadata.


III. U SE C ASE


The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX
100 with six axes from the Swiss company MABI Robotic
AG (Fig. 2 top). Due to its flexible rotary joints, high path
repeatability (0 _._ 06 mm) and positional accuracy (0 _._ 1 mm), as
well as its functional reach of 2 m, it is suitable for complex
manufacturing tasks such as machining [44]. The overarching
research task of this industrial robot is machining. The first
vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital
shadow.

Fig. 2 bottom sketches the functional structure. The first
axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor with
the current _I_ _M_ as input and torque _τ_ _M_ as output. To achieve
the necessary low gear ratio, a gear transmission is first used
(with torque _τ_ _G_ as output), followed by a cycloidal gear (with
torque _τ_ _out_ as output). The safety cell of the robot restricts the
maximum working range to [ _−_ 90 _[◦]_ _,_ 90 _[◦]_ ], and the maximum
achievable speed is _|q_ ˙ max _|_ = 75 _[◦]_ _/_ s. There are two position
encoders per drive. The motor encoder is mounted directly
on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox.
This sensor arrangement can reduce backlash and positional
uncertainty, as the difference between these encoders allows
conclusions to be drawn about the elasticity and friction of the
entire drive train.

The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D
sl. This Computer Numerical Control (CNC) controller is
particularly suitable for subtractive manufacturing processes
such as milling. The axes can be controlled separately. Position, speed, or acceleration can serve as inputs. Possible
outputs are the target and actual values of the position and its
derivatives, as well as the motor current. The motor current
can be converted into the applied torque using known motor

constants.


IV. A PPLICATION OF D IGITAL S HADOW C ONCEPTS IN

P RACTICE
